stories.title: Government tells Parliament it has no plan to scrap the LTCG tax on shares — ending months of investor rumours
key_points: Minister Pankaj Chaudhary: 'no proposal under consideration' to abolish it
LTCG on listed shares held over a year is taxed at 12.5%
Gains up to ₹1.25 lakh a year stay tax-free
Any change would come only in the annual Budget
stories.background: 'Long-term capital gains' (LTCG) tax is the tax you pay on the profit when you sell an investment you've held for a while. For listed Indian shares and equity mutual funds held more than a year, profits above ₹1.25 lakh in a year are taxed at 12.5%. Investors have long lobbied to scrap it, arguing it discourages long-term investing — and speculation had grown louder after the government recently gave foreign investors a tax break on government bonds, fuelling talk that domestic equity taxes might be eased too.
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, Minister of State for Finance Pankaj Chaudhary told the Lok Sabha (Parliament's lower house) that there is 'no proposal under consideration' to abolish LTCG tax on listed equities for retail and domestic investors. He added that capital-gains rates are reviewed only periodically, during the annual Budget, in light of the wider economy.
stories.why_it_matters: Tens of millions of ordinary Indians now invest in stocks and mutual funds, and this tax directly shapes their after-tax returns. The clarification kills a persistent rumour and signals policy continuity — useful because false 'the tax is going away' talk can distort how people time their buying and selling.
stories.ripple_effects: It removes a speculative overhang from equity-market sentiment and from the fast-growing mutual-fund industry. It also leaves alive a fairness debate: domestic equity investors still pay 12.5% while some foreign investors just won exemptions on government securities, and that gap will keep drawing calls for parity.
stories.why_now: The issue was raised in Parliament amid recurring investor demands and rumours that intensified after the recent tax breaks for foreign portfolio investors.
stories.watch_next: Any tweak to capital-gains rules in the next Union Budget, expected around February 2027 — the only realistic window for a change.

key_terms.definition: Tax on the profit from selling an asset held long enough to count as long-term — over one year for listed Indian shares.
An overseas investor who buys Indian stocks and bonds in financial markets, without taking control of companies.

stories.the_lesson: Investment tax rules almost never change outside the annual Budget, so mid-year 'they're about to scrap the tax' chatter is usually just noise — a reason to ignore rumours rather than trade on them.
stories.market_link: Neutral-to-supportive for broad Indian equities and mutual funds by removing a tax-uncertainty overhang; no single-stock impact.

stories.title: India says its capital spending has jumped five-fold to ₹90.87 lakh crore over 2014-26 — the engine behind its growth story
key_points: Effective capex ₹90.87 lakh crore in 2014-26 vs ₹17.04 lakh crore in 2004-14
The Centre's own direct capex rose to ₹64.70 lakh crore
₹26.17 lakh crore of that went as grants to states
Capex as a share of GDP doubled from 1.6% to 3.2%
stories.background: 'Capital expenditure' (capex) is money a government spends building things that last — roads, railways, ports, power lines — as opposed to day-to-day running costs or welfare handouts. Since around 2020, New Delhi has leaned hard on public capex as its main tool to drive growth, betting that building infrastructure creates jobs now and makes the whole economy more productive later. These figures were tabled in Parliament as the monsoon session got under way.
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, the Finance Ministry told Parliament that the Centre's 'effective' capital expenditure reached ₹90.87 lakh crore during 2014-26 — more than five times the ₹17.04 lakh crore spent in the previous decade (2004-14). Of that, ₹64.70 lakh crore was the Centre's own direct spending and ₹26.17 lakh crore was grants to states to build assets. Capex as a share of GDP rose from 1.6% in 2014-15 to 3.2% in 2023-24. ('Effective capex' bundles the Centre's own spending with the grants it gives states to build.)
stories.why_it_matters: This is the government's core answer to how India kept growing near 7-8% even through global shocks and an oil spike: instead of relying on consumers or exports, the state itself became the big spender, pouring money into infrastructure. Understanding this explains a lot of India's recent economic resilience.
stories.ripple_effects: Heavy public building directly feeds construction, cement, steel and capital-goods companies, and the jobs they create. But there's a trade-off: every rupee of capex adds to the fiscal deficit (government borrowing), which is harder to sustain when oil is dear and the rupee is weak. The open question is whether private companies will now invest enough to take over from the government.
stories.why_now: The data was placed before Parliament on the opening days of the monsoon session, with the government showcasing a record it wants credit for.
stories.watch_next: Whether private-sector investment finally picks up the baton, and whether the government can keep capex this high given fiscal pressure from costly oil.

key_terms.definition: Government spending on long-lasting assets like roads, railways and power grids, rather than on salaries, subsidies or interest.
An Indian unit for large numbers: 1 lakh crore = 1 trillion (₹90.87 lakh crore is about ₹90.87 trillion, or roughly $1.05 trillion).

stories.the_lesson: A government can power growth by spending on assets rather than handouts — building a road employs people today and lifts the economy's productivity for decades — but it works only as long as the borrowing behind it stays affordable.
stories.market_link: Supportive for infrastructure, cement, steel and capital-goods stocks (the likes of L&T, UltraTech and Larsen peers) and for the broader 'India growth' narrative that foreign investors watch.

stories.title: West Bengal's new BJP government makes Bengali compulsory for all official work from September 1
key_points: Bengali mandatory in state files, notices and police stations
Even letters to the Centre to be in Bengali (alongside Hindi)
First BJP CM Suvendu Adhikari fulfilling an election promise
About six weeks set aside to translate documents
stories.background: West Bengal, an eastern state of roughly 100 million people, is fiercely proud of its Bengali language and culture. In the 2026 assembly elections the BJP ended Mamata Banerjee's Trinamool Congress's long rule, and Suvendu Adhikari became the state's first-ever BJP chief minister in May 2026 — from a party rivals often accuse of favouring Hindi over regional tongues.
stories.what_happened: On July 20, the West Bengal government announced that from September 1, Bengali will be mandatory for all official government work — files, notifications, and communication at police stations — and that correspondence with the central government will be done in Bengali alongside Hindi. Officials will spend roughly the next six weeks translating necessary documents. The move fulfils a manifesto promise to preserve Bengali heritage and promote the language.
stories.why_it_matters: It is a striking political signal: a BJP government embracing regional-language pride to shed its 'outsider' image in Bengal, while stepping into India's sensitive language politics, where promoting one tongue over another can inflame identity and stir federal friction.
stories.ripple_effects: The rule reshapes day-to-day paperwork for lakhs (hundreds of thousands) of state employees and citizens, could sharpen the national Hindi-versus-regional-languages debate, and may pressure other states to assert their own languages — even as non-Bengali residents and businesses adapt.
stories.why_now: Weeks after taking power, the new government is moving early on cultural promises, and the announcement lands amid a wider national row over Bengali identity and the detention of Bengali-speaking migrant workers in other states.
stories.watch_next: How the September 1 rollout works in practice — translation readiness, and whether it faces legal or on-the-ground pushback.

key_terms.definition: The vote that picks a state's legislators, and thus its government and chief minister.
An Indian counting unit equal to 100,000.

stories.the_lesson: Language is rarely just about communication in India — controlling the official tongue is a way governments signal identity and win loyalty, so language policy is often politics by other means.

stories.title: Deadly monsoon floods hammer India's north and northeast — J&K toll climbs to 18 as the Army launches 'Operation Jal Rahat' in Assam
key_points: Jammu & Kashmir: at least 18 dead in Poonch and Rajouri
Cloudbursts washed away 20+ homes; several still missing
Army's 'Operation Jal Rahat' aids 57,000+ hit in Assam
IMD red alert for Himachal; rail traffic disrupted
stories.background: India's monsoon (June–September) delivers most of the year's rain — vital for farming, but capable of deadly flooding and landslides, especially in the Himalayan north and the low-lying northeast. 'Cloudbursts' — sudden, extremely intense downpours — can turn hill streams into torrents within minutes.
stories.what_happened: Torrential rain and cloudburst-triggered flash floods struck Jammu & Kashmir's border districts of Poonch and Rajouri on July 19; the death toll has since risen to 18 (about 15 in Poonch, 3 in Rajouri), with over 20 houses washed away and people still missing. In Assam, floods across seven districts have affected more than 57,000 people, prompting the Army and Assam Rifles to launch 'Operation Jal Rahat' ('Operation Water Relief'); the Air Force is on standby and rail services are disrupted. The weather office (IMD) issued a red alert for parts of Himachal Pradesh.
stories.why_it_matters: These are the deadliest days of this year's monsoon so far, striking fragile hill and border regions where roads, homes and rail links are quickly cut off — a stark reminder of how exposed India's terrain and infrastructure remain to sudden extreme rain.
stories.ripple_effects: Damaged tracks and roads disrupt travel and supplies; farmland and homes are lost just as the monsoon should be nourishing crops; relief and rebuilding cost states money; and repeated cloudbursts revive worries about Himalayan over-construction and climate-driven rainfall extremes.
stories.why_now: A pulse of intense monsoon rain hit the western Himalayas and the Brahmaputra basin over the same weekend, and India's 2026 monsoon has swung between drought and deluge — an erratic pattern scientists link to a building El Niño.
stories.watch_next: Whether the rain eases or the IMD's red alerts widen, and the final casualty and displacement counts as rescue operations continue.

key_terms.definition: A sudden, very heavy downpour over a small area, often triggering flash floods and landslides in hilly terrain.
The India Meteorological Department's highest warning level, meaning 'take action' against extremely heavy rain.

stories.the_lesson: In steep or low-lying terrain it isn't the total rainfall that kills but how fast it arrives — a cloudburst can drop a month's rain in an hour, outpacing any drainage or warning system.
stories.market_link: Severe monsoon damage can dent rural incomes and farm output, feeding food-price inflation and hitting demand for tractors, two-wheelers and rural lenders.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported cloudburst landslides in Nagaland and early flash floods in Kashmir as the monsoon turned violent across India's north and northeast.
thread.changed: Today the Kashmir toll has climbed to 18 in Poonch and Rajouri, and the Army has launched 'Operation Jal Rahat' in Assam, where floods now affect 57,000+ people, with a red alert issued for Himachal Pradesh.

prev_ref.title: Monsoon fury batters India's north and northeast — a deadly cloudburst landslide in Nagaland, flash floods in Kashmir

stories.title: A cash-theft scandal at the Ram temple reaches the Supreme Court — investigators report 8 arrests and 70 CCTV catches
key_points: A special team files its sealed status report in the Supreme Court
8 people arrested; 70 suspicious cash-hiding incidents caught on CCTV
Counting staff allegedly pocketed donation money at the Ayodhya temple
Petitioners want a CBI probe and a forensic audit of the trust
stories.background: The Ram Mandir (Ram temple) in Ayodhya, Uttar Pradesh, is one of India's most prominent religious sites, run by a government-linked trust (the Shri Ram Janmabhoomi Teerth Kshetra Trust) that handles enormous public donations. Allegations surfaced that some of the cash pilgrims donated was being stolen during counting. Petitions were filed asking courts to order an independent investigation and audit of the temple's money.
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, a Special Investigation Team (SIT) set up by the Uttar Pradesh government filed a status report in a sealed cover before a three-judge Supreme Court bench headed by Chief Justice Surya Kant. Investigators said eight people have been arrested so far, and that CCTV cameras captured around 70 suspicious incidents between April 27 and June 5, 2026, showing counting staff allegedly concealing wads of cash. The court has also issued notice to the temple trust seeking its response.
stories.why_it_matters: This is about trust in an institution millions of ordinary Indians donate to in good faith. When money given for a sacred cause is allegedly siphoned off, it raises hard questions about how public and religious funds are guarded — and whether the oversight is strong enough given the sheer amounts involved.
stories.ripple_effects: The case could force tighter financial controls, audits, and transparency rules on large temple trusts across India, which collectively handle thousands of crores. Politically, it is sensitive: the Ram temple is a flagship project associated with the ruling party, and opposition MPs have already raised the alleged embezzlement in Parliament, pressing for accountability.
stories.why_now: The Supreme Court had earlier asked the SIT to report back, and Monday was the scheduled hearing — landing on the same day Parliament's monsoon session opened, where the opposition is also raising the issue.
stories.watch_next: Whether the court orders a wider probe (such as a CBI investigation) or a forensic audit of the trust's finances after reviewing the sealed report.

key_terms.definition: A dedicated group of police officers formed to investigate one specific, often high-profile, case.
A confidential document handed to a court in a sealed envelope so its contents stay private until the judges decide to review or reveal them.
The Central Bureau of Investigation, India's premier federal investigating agency, often brought in for sensitive or nationwide cases.

stories.the_lesson: The bigger the pool of money an institution handles, the more it needs independent audits and cameras — trust alone doesn't guard cash; systems do.

stories.title: The rupee is inching toward a record low as costlier oil eats into India's finances
key_points: Rupee slips to about 96.42 per dollar, near its all-time low of 96.96
The Iran war has pushed oil back above $90 a barrel
India imports most of its oil, so a weak rupee makes it pricier still
A cheaper rupee feeds inflation and raises the import bill
stories.background: The rupee is India's currency, and its value is usually measured against the US dollar. India buys more than 80% of the oil it uses from abroad, and that oil is priced in dollars — so when oil gets expensive OR the rupee gets weaker, India's fuel bill jumps on both counts. The rupee has been sliding for weeks and touched an all-time low of 96.96 per dollar back in May.
stories.what_happened: In early trade on Monday, July 20, the rupee fell about 12 paise to roughly 96.42 against the US dollar, leaving it within striking distance of its record low. Traders blame a fresh surge in oil prices — Brent crude is back above $90 a barrel — driven by the escalating US–Iran war that has choked the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow sea lane through which much of the world's oil moves. The currency has now dropped about 1.7% this month.
stories.why_it_matters: A weaker rupee is not an abstract number — it quietly raises the cost of almost everything India buys from abroad: crude oil, electronics, medicines, edible oils, and foreign travel or education. That feeds into inflation (the general rise in prices), squeezing household budgets, and it makes India's import bill balloon just as oil is already expensive.
stories.ripple_effects: If Brent crude pushes past $95, analysts warn the rupee could break to a fresh all-time low. The Reserve Bank of India (RBI), the country's central bank, may spend some of its foreign-currency reserves selling dollars to slow the fall — a costly defence. A weaker rupee also nudges up the price of imported goods for companies and can dent foreign investors' returns, sometimes prompting them to pull money out of Indian stocks, which pressures the market further.
stories.why_now: The trigger is the intensifying US–Iran conflict, which has disrupted traffic through the Strait of Hormuz and sent oil prices climbing again — a direct hit to big energy importers like India.
stories.watch_next: Whether Brent crude crosses $95 a barrel and whether the rupee breaches its 96.96 record low in the coming sessions.

key_terms.definition: A narrow sea passage between Iran and Oman through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil is shipped; disruptions there quickly push oil prices up.
The smaller unit of the rupee — 100 paise make one rupee; a '12 paise' fall means the rupee lost 0.12 of a rupee against the dollar.
A country's stockpile of foreign money (mostly dollars) and gold, which a central bank can sell to steady its own currency.

stories.the_lesson: For a country that imports most of its energy, its currency and the oil price are tied together — when oil rises, the currency often falls, and the pain lands twice on the import bill.
stories.market_link: A weaker rupee and pricey oil pressure oil-marketing companies, importers, and rate-sensitive stocks; it can also trigger foreign selling in the Sensex and Nifty.

stories.title: India's 'yo-yo' monsoon — drought, then deluge, then drought — threatens crops and the power grid as a 'super' El Niño builds
key_points: Fifth-driest June on record (~40% below normal), then a sudden deluge
Now stalled again — rainfall ~24% below normal as of July 19
Erratic rain hits rain-fed crops: rice, pulses, oilseeds, cotton, sugar
A strong El Niño could stress India's power grid more than any nation's
stories.background: About half of India's farmland has no irrigation and depends entirely on the June–September monsoon rains, and those rains also refill the reservoirs that run hydro power and cool thermal plants. El Niño is a periodic warming of the Pacific Ocean that tends to weaken India's monsoon and push up temperatures. When the rains arrive in erratic bursts instead of a steady spread, both farming and electricity get squeezed at once.
stories.what_happened: Analysts and a report by the Centre for Research on Energy and Clean Air (CREA) warn that an intensifying El Niño, amplified by global warming, is making India's 2026 monsoon lurch between extremes. The season opened with the fifth-driest June on record — rain almost 40% below normal — then dumped roughly a month's rain on cities like Mumbai in a single week, before stalling into another dry break that left national rainfall about 24% below normal by July 19. Scientists say the El Niño could be among the strongest on record, peaking between late 2026 and early 2027.
stories.why_it_matters: Farming employs a huge share of Indians, so a bad monsoon dents rural incomes and can spike food prices for everyone. At the same time, hotter, drier spells push up electricity demand for cooling while leaving less water to generate it — raising the risk of power cuts. CREA warns India's power sector may be more exposed to El Niño than any other country's.
stories.ripple_effects: Weaker harvests can lift prices of rice, pulses, sugar and edible oils, feeding food inflation that the Reserve Bank of India watches when setting interest rates. Poor rains sap rural spending on everything from soap to motorcycles, hitting consumer-goods companies. The government may curb exports (as India has done before with rice and sugar) to protect domestic supply, which ripples into world food markets.
stories.why_now: The warning is surfacing as the monsoon's mid-season 'break' drags on and forecasters upgrade the El Niño outlook — the window when the crucial kharif (monsoon-sown) crop is being planted and any rainfall shortfall does the most damage.
stories.watch_next: Whether August rains recover the deficit, and the India Meteorological Department's updated monsoon and El Niño forecasts.

key_terms.definition: A periodic warming of the tropical Pacific Ocean that often weakens India's monsoon and raises temperatures worldwide.
Crops such as rice, cotton, soybean and maize that are sown with the arrival of the June monsoon and harvested in autumn.

stories.the_lesson: For farming and power, the timing of rain matters as much as the total: a monsoon that swings between drought and deluge can do more damage than one that is simply a bit below average, because crops and grids need steady water, not feast-or-famine.
stories.market_link: A weak or erratic monsoon pressures consumer-staples and agri-input stocks, can lift sugar and rice prices, and raises power-shortage risk — while a resurgent RBI inflation worry can delay interest-rate cuts.

stories.title: Modi opens Parliament by touting 7.7% growth 'despite' the Middle East war — India's economy holds up even as oil bites
key_points: PM Modi: India still grew 7.7%, the fastest of any big economy
He framed it as strength 'despite' the West Asia (Iran–US) war
That war has pushed oil past $90 — costly for import-reliant India
Said at the chaotic first day of Parliament's monsoon session
stories.background: India buys most of its crude oil, cooking gas, fertiliser and many chemicals from abroad — so when war in the Middle East (which the government calls 'West Asia') pushes energy prices up, India's import bill swells and prices at home can rise. GDP (Gross Domestic Product) is the total value of everything a country produces in a year; its growth rate is the headline measure of how fast the economy is expanding. India has been the fastest-growing large economy for a few years, and the government leans on that number as proof its policies are working.
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, addressing the media as Parliament's monsoon session opened, Prime Minister Narendra Modi said India had maintained a 7.7% growth rate and remained the world's fastest-growing major economy, calling it a reflection of the country's 'inherent strength.' He explicitly tied the achievement to the strain of the Middle East war, noting India depends on others for petrol, diesel, LPG, fertilisers and chemicals, and urged all MPs to make the session productive.
stories.why_it_matters: A 7.7% pace, if it holds, means jobs, tax revenue and rising incomes even while a war disrupts global energy — a genuinely strong number by world standards. But the framing is also political: the session opens amid opposition uproar over exam-paper leaks, so Modi is setting an economic-confidence tone before the fights begin. The real test is whether growth survives if oil stays high.
stories.ripple_effects: Pricier oil widens India's trade gap and pressures the rupee, which slipped to about 96.42 per US dollar on the same day; a weaker rupee makes imports dearer and can feed inflation. If fuel and fertiliser costs keep climbing, the government may face pressure to cut fuel taxes or raise subsidies — spending that strains the budget. For ordinary people it shows up as costlier petrol, transport and food.
stories.why_now: The remark came at the ceremonial start of the monsoon session, the traditional moment a PM frames the national mood — and it landed just as the US–Iran war entered a ninth night of strikes and crude pushed above $90, making 'growth despite the war' the government's chosen headline.
stories.watch_next: Whether the next official GDP and inflation prints confirm the 7.7% pace, and how high India's oil-import bill climbs if the Gulf conflict drags on.

key_terms.definition: How fast the total value of everything a country produces is expanding, usually measured year over year — the headline gauge of economic health.
A country's reliance on buying oil, gas and fuel from abroad, which leaves it exposed when global prices spike.

stories.the_lesson: A country that imports most of its energy is financially tied to distant wars: a conflict thousands of miles away can raise its fuel prices, weaken its currency and shrink its growth — which is why 'growth despite oil' is a genuine test of economic resilience.
stories.market_link: Higher crude and a weaker rupee pressure Indian equities (the Sensex fell ~590 points the same morning), oil marketers and import-heavy sectors, while boosting upstream oil producers like ONGC.

stories.title: India's switch to E20 petrol sparks a driver backlash — clogged injectors, lower mileage and a political row
key_points: Petrol pumps now sell E20 — fuel with 20% ethanol blended in — almost everywhere
Owners report lower mileage, clogged filters and fuel-system trouble, mostly in older cars
The government moved its 20% blending target forward from 2030 to 2025
Minister Nitin Gadkari says testing shows no engine damage; critics disagree
stories.background: Ethanol is an alcohol-based fuel made from crops like sugarcane; blending it into petrol is meant to cut India's costly oil imports, support farmers, and lower some emissions. 'E20' means the petrol is 20% ethanol, 80% regular petrol. Under its National Policy on Biofuels, the Modi government pulled its 20%-blending goal forward by five years — from 2030 to 2025 — and E20 is now the standard at pumps nationwide.
stories.what_happened: As E20 has rolled out, vehicle owners across the country have complained of lower fuel efficiency, clogged injectors and filters, swollen or cracked rubber parts, and worries about long-term engine wear — problems reported mostly in pre-2023 vehicles not designed for higher ethanol blends. Road Transport Minister Nitin Gadkari has dismissed the concerns, saying E20 was introduced only after extensive testing and that the government has received no verified complaints of engine damage. The row deepened after the government's own attorney general told the Supreme Court in June that the Centre was 'experimenting' with the blend — remarks the government later said were misunderstood.
stories.why_it_matters: This touches tens of millions of ordinary vehicle owners in their wallets — through mileage and repair bills — which is why a technical fuel policy has become a political controversy. It's a case study in the trade-off between a big national goal (energy security, less oil import) and the immediate, uneven costs borne by people whose older cars weren't built for the new fuel.
stories.ripple_effects: Sustained anger could force the government to offer clearer guidance, compatibility labelling, or a non-ethanol option, and it boosts demand for engine-protection products and servicing. It's a tailwind for sugar and ethanol producers (distilleries) and shapes how automakers tune future engines; oil marketing companies must manage supply and customer trust.
stories.why_now: With E20 now the default at pumps and complaints piling up online, explainers and political attacks are peaking — and the topic is resurfacing as Parliament's monsoon session opens and the opposition looks for consumer-pocket issues.
stories.watch_next: Whether the government mandates clearer E20 labelling or compatibility guidance, and any official study or Supreme Court direction on engine-damage claims.

key_terms.definition: Mixing plant-based alcohol (ethanol) into petrol to stretch fuel supply, cut oil imports and support crop farmers.
When ethanol in fuel absorbs water and splits away from the petrol, which can corrode parts and hurt engine performance.

stories.the_lesson: Big energy transitions rarely come free — a policy that helps the country as a whole (less imported oil) can still land real costs on individuals, and how a government handles those costs decides whether the switch is trusted or resented.
stories.market_link: Positive for sugar and ethanol producers (e.g. distilleries); a management and PR issue for oil marketing companies and automakers.

stories.title: Tripura's police chief is found dead in his office — police suspect he shot himself with his service pistol
key_points: DGP Anurag Dhankar was found dead at the state police headquarters in Agartala
Early findings point to suicide with his own service pistol; a probe is on
A 1994-batch IPS officer, he became Tripura's police chief in May 2025
At the CBI he had led the probe into the Nirav Modi bank-fraud case
stories.background: The Director General of Police (DGP) is the top uniformed officer in an Indian state — the person who runs its entire police force. Anurag Dhankar was a 1994-batch officer of the Indian Police Service (IPS), the elite all-India cadre that staffs senior police posts. He had a high-profile career, including a long stint at the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI), India's top federal investigating agency, where he led the investigation into fugitive diamond merchant Nirav Modi's alleged multi-crore bank fraud.
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, Dhankar was found dead inside his office at the Tripura police headquarters in Agartala. Preliminary indications, according to police, are that he may have died by suicide using his service pistol, though an investigation has been launched to establish exactly what happened. His body was moved to GB Pant Hospital in Agartala for a post-mortem examination.
stories.why_it_matters: A serving state police chief dying in office — apparently by his own weapon — is rare and jolting, and it raises hard questions about the pressures and mental health of even the most senior officials. Until the probe finishes, the cause is not confirmed, and how the state handles the investigation of its own top cop will be closely watched.
stories.ripple_effects: Tripura will need to name an acting DGP quickly to avoid a leadership gap at the top of its police. Expect scrutiny of any personal or professional stress, security protocols for officers' service weapons, and renewed conversation about mental-health support for police and bureaucrats.
stories.why_now: The death is breaking news today; details are still emerging and officials are cautioning that the exact circumstances are unconfirmed pending the post-mortem and inquiry.
stories.watch_next: The post-mortem findings and the official inquiry report on the cause of death, and the appointment of an interim DGP.

key_terms.definition: The highest-ranking police officer in an Indian state, who heads the entire state police force.
The national civil service that supplies India's senior police officers, recruited through a tough all-India exam.

stories.the_lesson: Even the most powerful officials operate under intense, often invisible pressure — and in a live story like a sudden death, the responsible move is to report what's confirmed and clearly flag what is still only 'suspected' until an inquiry settles it.

stories.title: India's IT giants pull back on fresh graduates — delayed joining dates, quietly withdrawn offers, and a salary-hike rethink
key_points: IT firms delaying joining dates and withdrawing campus offers
Accenture overhauls salary hikes after weak results
Hiring shifts toward specialised, AI-ready skills
Freshers left waiting with uncertain start dates
stories.background: India's roughly $250-billion IT services industry — TCS, Infosys, Wipro, and global players like Accenture — has for decades been the country's great white-collar job engine, hiring hundreds of thousands of engineering graduates a year. But two forces are squeezing it: soft demand from Western clients, and AI tools that can now do chunks of the entry-level coding and support work freshers used to be hired for. A weak set of quarterly results earlier this month (the 'IT bloodbath') already spooked the sector.
stories.what_happened: Reports say IT companies are revisiting their campus-hiring playbook: joining dates for graduates who already hold offers are being pushed back, some roles are being quietly withdrawn, and start dates are being tied to shifting business demand. Separately, Accenture overhauled its salary-hike structure — reworking how raises are handed out after disappointing results. The common thread: firms are hiring fewer generalist freshers and leaning toward specialised, AI-ready skills.
stories.why_it_matters: This hits millions of young Indians directly. The IT job was the reliable ladder from an engineering degree into the middle class; if that ladder narrows, it changes career bets, family finances, and even which courses students choose. It is also an early, concrete example of AI reshaping who gets hired, not just abstract fear about it.
stories.ripple_effects: Engineering colleges may see placement numbers dip and pivot curricula toward AI and cloud skills. Coaching and upskilling firms benefit as graduates scramble to specialise. Consumer spending among young professionals could soften. And it pressures IT stocks, whose margins investors are watching closely amid the global AI-driven tech re-rating.
stories.why_now: It follows a weak June-quarter earnings season and comes as AI tools mature fast enough to absorb routine entry-level work — forcing firms to rethink the old hire-and-train model.
stories.watch_next: Whether the big three — TCS, Infosys, Wipro — confirm smaller fresher intakes in their next hiring guidance, and how long deferred joiners are made to wait.

key_terms.definition: Recruiting students directly from colleges, often months before they graduate, with a promised joining date later.
The process of formally bringing a hired employee into a company and getting them started in the role.

stories.the_lesson: When a new technology can do the easiest tasks in a field, the entry-level rung is the first to shrink — which reshapes who gets hired long before it changes senior roles.
stories.market_link: Pressures Indian IT stocks (TCS, Infosys, Wipro) and Accenture; part of the broader AI-driven re-rating weighing on the Nifty IT index.

stories.title: Sensex sinks 700 points as HDFC Bank's shrinking margins and pricey oil hit Dalal Street
key_points: Sensex fell as much as 706 points; Nifty near 24,150
HDFC Bank slid up to 5% on weaker margins
Rupee slipped to a two-month low of 96.42/$
Brent crude topping $90 added to the pressure
stories.background: The Sensex and Nifty 50 are India's two main stock-market indexes — baskets of big companies whose combined price shows how the market is doing. Banks carry heavy weight in both, so when a giant like HDFC Bank (India's largest private lender) moves, the whole index moves. A bank's 'net interest margin' (NIM) is the gap between what it earns on loans and what it pays on deposits — its core profitability. Markets had also been jittery for days over the US-Iran conflict pushing up oil.
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, the Sensex dropped as much as 706 points (about 0.9%) to an intraday low around 77,445, while the Nifty 50 fell about 184 points to roughly 24,150. The trigger was HDFC Bank: its June-quarter profit was overshadowed by a sharper-than-expected fall in net interest margins, and the stock slid up to 5%, dragging Axis Bank, Kotak and the wider banking index down as brokerages flagged softer margins sector-wide. On top of that, Brent crude climbing past $90 weakened the rupee to a two-month low of 96.42 per dollar and spurred foreign investors to pull money out.
stories.why_it_matters: Banks are the engine of the index, so a margin scare at the biggest private lender rattles the whole market and dents the savings of millions of Indian investors and mutual-fund holders. A weaker rupee makes imports — especially oil — costlier, feeding inflation. It shows how a global oil shock and a domestic earnings miss can combine to knock the market on the same day.
stories.ripple_effects: Costlier crude and a falling rupee squeeze oil-importing companies and can force the Reserve Bank of India to spend reserves defending the currency. Foreign investors selling Indian shares ('FII outflows') can deepen the slide. If bank margins keep shrinking across the sector, it caps how much the market's biggest weight can rise, keeping a lid on indexes even if other sectors do well.
stories.why_now: Monday was the market's first session to digest both HDFC Bank's weekend results and the fresh spike in oil from the escalating US-Iran conflict.
stories.watch_next: Whether other big banks report the same margin squeeze this earnings season, and how far the rupee slides if oil stays above $90.

key_terms.definition: The difference between the interest a bank earns on its loans and what it pays out on deposits — a core gauge of how profitable its lending is.
Money that foreign institutional investors pull out of a country's stocks and bonds, which can push local markets and the currency down.

stories.the_lesson: In an index dominated by a few heavyweight banks, one lender's margin miss can sink the whole market — concentration means a single stock's problem becomes everyone's.
stories.market_link: HDFC Bank, Axis Bank and Kotak led the fall; a weak rupee and $90 oil pressure importers and the broader Nifty, while oil marketers and rate-sensitive sectors stay in focus.

stories.title: 'Cockroach' march to Parliament turns violent — police baton-charge protesters in Delhi
key_points: CJP's 'Sansad Chalo' march reached central Delhi
Police used tear gas and baton-charges near Parliament Street
Officers and protesters injured; several detained
Founder Abhijeet Dipke ended his hunger strike
stories.background: The Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) is a new Gen-Z-led movement born out of anger over repeated leaks of India's public exams — the tests millions of young people take for government jobs and college places. It had called a 'Sansad Chalo' ('March to Parliament') for July 20, the opening day of Parliament's monsoon session. Delhi Police had already declared the march unauthorised and invoked BNSS Section 163 (a legal order that bans large gatherings), while activist Sonam Wangchuk and CJP figures backed the cause.
stories.what_happened: On Monday, July 20, thousands of CJP supporters tried to march from Jantar Mantar toward Parliament. Police, who had shut mobile internet in parts of central Delhi and deployed heavy barricades, fired tear-gas shells and baton-charged ('lathi-charged') the crowd near Parliament Street and Shastri Bhawan after protesters allegedly tried to breach the barricades. Several police officers and protesters were injured and many demonstrators were detained. CJP founder Abhijeet Dipke ended his hunger strike, and party representatives went to meet Union Minister J.P. Nadda; Wangchuk, kept in hospital by a court order, was represented by his wife Gitanjali Angmo.
stories.why_it_matters: This turns a planned protest into a live confrontation between a youth movement and the state on the very day Parliament opened — sharpening a national fight over exam integrity and jobs. How the government handles a leaderless, social-media-driven Gen-Z movement sets a template for future youth protests. Injuries and internet shutdowns in the capital also raise civil-liberties questions.
stories.ripple_effects: The opposition is already using the exam-leak issue to corner the government inside Parliament, so street clashes amplify the political pressure on Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan. Repeated internet shutdowns and baton-charges draw criticism from rights groups and can harden the movement rather than defuse it. Expect the government's outreach (the Nadda meeting) to be read as either a genuine olive branch or an attempt to split the protest.
stories.why_now: The march was timed for the first day of the monsoon session to maximise attention, and the clash unfolded as protesters pushed against the security cordon on Monday afternoon.
stories.watch_next: Whether the CJP-government talks yield any concession on exam reform, and if the movement calls fresh protests after the crackdown.

key_terms.definition: A provision of India's new criminal-procedure code that lets authorities ban gatherings and impose restrictions in an area — the successor to the old 'Section 144'.
When police use long wooden batons ('lathis') to beat back and disperse a crowd.

stories.the_lesson: Leaderless, online-organised youth movements are hard for governments to negotiate with or shut down — banning a march can turn a grievance into a bigger confrontation rather than ending it.

thread.previously: Delhi Police had declared the CJP's July 20 march to Parliament illegal, saying no permission was granted, while a hospitalised Sonam Wangchuk refused to break his fast.
thread.changed: The march went ahead anyway: police fired tear gas and baton-charged protesters near Parliament Street, injuring officers and demonstrators, and founder Abhijeet Dipke ended his hunger strike as talks with the government began.

prev_ref.title: Delhi Police declare the 'Cockroach' party's July 20 march to Parliament illegal as a hospitalised Wangchuk refuses to break his fast

stories.title: Reliance beats forecasts with a record quarter — ₹23,001 crore profit on strong refining and Jio, and brokerages turn bullish
key_points: Q1 net profit ₹23,001 crore — ahead of analysts' estimates
Driven by strong oil-refining (O2C) and telecom arm Jio; retail softer
JPMorgan: 'Finally, a beat'; Goldman Sachs sees ~41% upside
Shares rose ~1% even as the wider market fell
stories.background: Reliance Industries is India's most valuable listed company and a bellwether — a stock big enough that its results move the whole market's mood. It spans three main businesses: 'O2C' (oil-to-chemicals, i.e. refining crude oil and making petrochemicals), Jio (its telecom and digital arm), and retail. In recent quarters the company had underwhelmed investors, so this result was closely watched. India's April–June quarter is the first of its 2026-27 financial year (Q1FY27).
stories.what_happened: Reliance reported a June-quarter net profit of about ₹23,001 crore (roughly $2.7 billion), beating analysts' forecasts, alongside record quarterly core profit and EBITDA (a measure of operating earnings). Strong oil-refining margins and Jio's growth drove the beat, offsetting a softer retail performance. Brokerages responded warmly — JPMorgan headlined 'Finally, a beat', while Goldman Sachs stayed bullish and pointed to about 41% potential upside in the share price. The stock opened near ₹1,317 and traded up around 1% even as India's benchmark indexes fell.
stories.why_it_matters: Because Reliance is so heavily weighted in India's stock indexes, its results shape sentiment for millions of investors and mutual-fund holders. A clear earnings 'beat' after a run of disappointments is a confidence signal — and the fact that the stock rose while the broader market dropped shows investors singling it out as a relative safe haven on a rough day.
stories.ripple_effects: Bullish brokerage targets can pull more buyers into the stock and steady the wider market, since Reliance's weight cushions index falls. Strong refining margins hint that Reliance is profiting from the same high oil prices that are hurting India's economy elsewhere (a weaker rupee, costlier fuel). Jio's momentum keeps pressure on rival telecom firms, and any capital-spending plans Reliance signals ripple through its suppliers and partners.
stories.why_now: Companies report quarterly results on a set calendar; Reliance released its Q1FY27 numbers overnight, and the market reaction and brokerage notes are landing this morning.
stories.watch_next: Whether other index heavyweights' results this week reinforce or undercut the 'beat', and where brokerages set their revised price targets for Reliance.

key_terms.definition: Earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation — a rough measure of a company's core operating profit before financing and accounting costs.
An Indian unit equal to ten million (1 crore = 1,00,00,000); ₹23,001 crore is about ₹230 billion, or roughly $2.7 billion.

stories.the_lesson: A single dominant company can move an entire index — when one stock is heavily weighted in a benchmark, its earnings 'beat' or 'miss' colours the whole market's day, regardless of how other companies did.
stories.market_link: Reliance Industries shares directly, plus the Nifty 50 and Sensex (which it heavily influences) and telecom peers like Bharti Airtel and Vodafone Idea.

stories.title: Parliament's monsoon session opens in chaos — both Houses adjourned as the Opposition storms over exam-paper leaks
key_points: Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha both adjourned soon after opening
Opposition demands a debate on question-paper leaks and student protests
Outside, police lathi-charged 'Cockroach' (CJP) marchers near Parliament
PM Modi appealed for 'a productive session'; it runs until August 13
stories.background: India's Parliament has two chambers — the Lok Sabha (lower house, directly elected) and the Rajya Sabha (upper house). It meets in fixed 'sessions', and the monsoon session is the mid-year one. For weeks, anger has built over leaked exam question papers — recruitment and entrance tests whose answers escaped in advance, wrecking the chances of honest students — and a linked youth protest movement (the 'Cockroach'/CJP campaign) demanding accountability. The Opposition had signalled it would use the session to corner the government and demand Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation.
stories.what_happened: On the session's first day, Monday, July 20, both Houses were adjourned shortly after convening as Opposition members protested and demanded a formal discussion on the paper leaks and the student agitation. Prime Minister Narendra Modi, in his customary pre-session address, said he hoped 'both the monsoon and the Monsoon Session are productive' and praised young Indian innovators — while taking a veiled swipe at a '56-year-old youth', widely read as a jab at Opposition leader Rahul Gandhi. Outside Parliament, police lathi-charged (baton-charged) and detained members of the 'Chalo Sansad' (Let's go to Parliament) march near Jantar Mantar, where prohibitory orders were in force.
stories.why_it_matters: Parliament is where India's laws are made and the government is held to account, so how the session runs shapes what actually gets passed. A first day lost to adjournments signals a combative session ahead, with the government pushing contentious bills and the Opposition determined to disrupt over the leaks. It also shows a rare crossover: street protest and parliamentary politics feeding each other over the same grievance — the credibility of India's exam system.
stories.ripple_effects: Repeated adjournments can stall or rush important legislation (bills may pass with little debate amid the din). The exam-leak row keeps pressure on the education ministry and the testing agencies, and could force reforms to how exams are run. The heavy-handed policing of the youth march risks inflaming the very movement the government wants to defuse, and hands the Opposition a fresh talking point.
stories.why_now: The monsoon session opened today, July 20, giving both the Opposition and the street movement a national stage on the same morning — which is why the confrontation is peaking now.
stories.watch_next: Whether the government agrees to a formal debate on the paper leaks in the coming days — and which contentious bills it tries to introduce while the Houses are disrupted.

key_terms.definition: Pausing or ending a day's parliamentary sitting; frequent adjournments mean little actual business gets done.
When police use long wooden batons (lathis) to disperse a crowd.

stories.the_lesson: A legislature can be paralysed without a single vote — controlling the floor through protest and adjournment is itself a political tool, used by oppositions to force an issue onto the agenda.

thread.previously: Yesterday we said the Opposition would use the monsoon session's opening to demand Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation over the exam-leak scandal, with a youth 'Cockroach' march planned for Parliament.
thread.changed: Today it played out: both Houses were adjourned amid Opposition uproar on day one, and police lathi-charged and detained marchers outside Parliament.

prev_ref.title: Exam-leak anger reaches Parliament: opposition will demand Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan quit as the monsoon session opens tomorrow

stories.title: ICICI Bank's profit rises 16% to ₹14,805 crore — India's second-biggest private bank posts another strong quarter
key_points: Q1 net profit up ~16% to ₹14,805 crore
Lower provisions (money set aside for bad loans) helped
Latest in a run of solid private-bank results
stories.background: India's private banks have been on a strong run, powered by fast loan growth in a fast-growing economy and relatively low bad loans. ICICI Bank is the country's second-largest private-sector lender (after HDFC Bank) and a heavyweight in the Nifty 50 index, so its results are read as a gauge of the whole sector's health. This covers Q1 of India's 2026-27 financial year — the April–June quarter.
stories.what_happened: ICICI reported net profit (PAT, or profit after tax) of about ₹14,805 crore, up roughly 16% from a year earlier. Provisions — the buffer a bank sets aside to cover loans that might go bad — fell to about ₹1,260 crore from ₹1,815 crore a year ago, meaning fewer expected defaults ate into profit.
stories.why_it_matters: A big, closely-watched bank growing profit by double digits while its bad-loan buffer shrinks suggests India's credit engine and its borrowers are still healthy. Banks are the backbone of the stock market — they carry heavy weight in the Nifty — so their earnings shape the overall index and market mood.
stories.ripple_effects: Strong bank results support the Nifty and can lift other financial stocks. ICICI adds to a picture of resilient private banks this season (Kotak +26%, Axis +23%, Yes Bank +34%), reassuring foreign investors even amid global oil-and-war jitters. Healthy banks also mean credit keeps flowing to businesses and homebuyers.
stories.why_now: It is Q1 earnings season — India's big banks report their April–June results in mid-to-late July, and ICICI's landed over the weekend.
stories.watch_next: HDFC Bank and the rest of the sector's results, plus whether rising global risks (oil, war) begin to slow loan demand.

key_terms.definition: A company's bottom-line profit once all costs and taxes are paid — the headline 'profit' number.
Money a bank sets aside in advance to cover loans it expects might not be repaid; lower provisions usually mean it sees fewer bad loans ahead.
A bank's core earnings — the gap between the interest it earns on loans and the interest it pays on deposits.

stories.the_lesson: For a bank, falling 'provisions' can flatter profit: when a lender sets aside less for bad loans, earnings rise — so always check whether growth comes from real business or just a smaller safety buffer.
stories.market_link: Supportive for ICICI Bank shares and the Bank Nifty; bank earnings drive a large share of Nifty 50 moves.

stories.title: India tells its citizens to leave Iran and postpone travel there as the US–Iran war escalates
key_points: MEA advisory: postpone all non-essential travel to Iran
Indians already in Iran urged to leave or stay extremely cautious
Follows days of US airstrikes and Iranian retaliation
stories.background: India has deep ties to the Gulf: millions of Indians live and work across the region, and India buys much of its oil from there. When Middle East conflicts flare, New Delhi's first job is protecting its nationals — it has run large evacuations before, such as from Ukraine in 2022 and Yemen in 2015. With the US–Iran fight now escalating sharply, Iran has become dangerous for foreigners.
stories.what_happened: India's Ministry of External Affairs (MEA) issued a travel advisory urging citizens to postpone non-essential travel to Iran and asking those already there to leave or exercise extreme caution, citing repeated US airstrikes and Iranian retaliation. It is a precautionary step — short of a full, organised evacuation.
stories.why_it_matters: It signals that New Delhi believes the conflict could worsen quickly, and this kind of advisory is usually the move that precedes larger evacuation efforts. For thousands of Indian workers, students and pilgrims in the region, it is an immediate, personal warning.
stories.ripple_effects: If the fighting spreads, India may have to mount an evacuation across the wider Gulf, not just Iran. The war also hits India's economy directly: higher oil prices widen India's import bill, can weaken the rupee and stoke inflation — a risk the RBI Governor has flagged for growth. India also operates Chabahar port in Iran, a key trade and connectivity route, now under a cloud.
stories.why_now: The advisory came within hours of the conflict escalating into direct US–Iran strikes and Iran hitting Gulf infrastructure, raising the danger to foreign nationals inside Iran.
stories.watch_next: Whether India upgrades this into an organised evacuation, and any disruption to oil shipments that would push up prices at home.

key_terms.definition: An official government warning about the risks of visiting a country, ranging from 'be cautious' to 'leave now'.
India's foreign ministry, which handles diplomacy and the safety of Indian citizens abroad.

stories.the_lesson: A travel advisory is an early-warning siren: governments issue them before they are forced to evacuate, so a 'postpone travel / leave now' notice often means officials fear things are about to get worse.
stories.market_link: Negative for the rupee and oil-importers; higher crude widens India's trade deficit and pressures the Nifty, while domestic oil producers like ONGC can benefit.

stories.title: India Inc's mandatory 'giving' hits a record ₹40,794 crore — the money big companies are legally required to spend on society jumps 17%
key_points: Corporate social spending hit a record ₹40,794 crore in FY25, up 17%
Education got the biggest slice — ₹13,877 crore (34%)
Healthcare was second at ₹8,531 crore (21%)
India is the rare country that makes such spending a legal duty
stories.background: 'CSR' stands for Corporate Social Responsibility — money companies spend on social causes like schools, hospitals and clean water. What makes India unusual is that this is not optional: under the Companies Act of 2013, India became the first major country to legally require it. Big firms — roughly those with net worth over ₹500 crore, sales over ₹1,000 crore, or profit over ₹5 crore — must spend at least 2% of their average recent profits on approved social projects every year, or explain why they didn't.
stories.what_happened: A new industry report (the Bharat CSR Performance Report 2026, by research firm Fulcrum) found that India's companies spent a record ₹40,794 crore on CSR in the 2024-25 financial year — a 17% rise on the year before. Education drew the most money at ₹13,877 crore (about 34% of the total), followed by healthcare at ₹8,531 crore (21%). Geographically, Maharashtra received the most (₹8,631 crore), while Gujarat saw the fastest growth, with inflows up 68%.
stories.why_it_matters: This is a large, guaranteed river of private money flowing into public goods — bigger than many government scheme budgets — and it grows automatically as corporate profits rise. The 17% jump signals both healthier company earnings (the 2% is a slice of profit) and rising compliance. Where that money goes — education, health, which states — shapes real services for millions.
stories.ripple_effects: Charities and non-profits compete hard for these corporate funds, and the tilt toward education and healthcare steers where new schools and clinics get built. The heavy concentration in richer states like Maharashtra and Gujarat — rather than India's poorest regions — fuels a long-running debate about whether mandatory giving actually reaches the areas that need it most.
stories.why_now: FY25 results and disclosures are now finalised, so full-year CSR totals are being tallied and published — an annual snapshot of how much corporate India gave and where.
stories.watch_next: Whether future years narrow the gap so more CSR money flows to India's poorer, less-industrialised states rather than clustering where big companies are already based.

key_terms.definition: Money and effort companies put into social good — India legally requires large firms to spend at least 2% of average profits on it.
The Indian law governing companies; it introduced the world's first broad legal mandate for corporate social spending.

stories.the_lesson: A rule that ties charitable spending to a share of profits turns social funding into an automatic, growing stream — but it tends to pool where big companies already sit, so a mandate to give doesn't guarantee the money reaches those who need it most.

stories.title: Yes Bank — the lender that nearly collapsed in 2020 — posts a 34% jump in quarterly profit as its turnaround gathers pace
key_points: Net profit up ~34% to ₹1,071 crore for the June quarter
Core interest income rose 17.5%; loans grew 18.3%
Bad-loan ratio eased to 1.3% from 1.6% a year ago
A milestone for a bank rescued from failure in 2020
stories.background: Yes Bank was one of India's fastest-growing private banks until March 2020, when the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) — the country's banking regulator — seized it after a pile-up of bad loans left it near collapse, briefly freezing customers' withdrawals. A State Bank of India-led group of lenders pumped in rescue capital and installed new management. The years since have been a slow rebuild: cleaning up bad loans, rebuilding deposits and restoring trust. Each solid quarter is a check on whether the turnaround is real.
stories.what_happened: For the April–June quarter (the first of India's 2026-27 financial year), Yes Bank reported standalone net profit of about ₹1,071 crore, up roughly 34% from ₹801 crore a year earlier, beating analysts' forecasts. Net interest income — the gap between what a bank earns on loans and pays on deposits, a bank's core engine — rose 17.5% to ₹2,786 crore. Loans grew 18.3% to ₹2.85 lakh crore, and the gross bad-loan (NPA) ratio fell to 1.3% from 1.6%.
stories.why_it_matters: A 34% profit jump with falling bad loans is exactly the shape of a healthy recovery — the bank is lending more, earning more on it, and losing less to defaults. For a lender that regulators had to rescue five years ago, it is evidence the clean-up is working and that Yes Bank is edging back toward being an ordinary, profitable bank.
stories.ripple_effects: Steady profits make it easier for Yes Bank to raise capital and grow lending, and they matter to the millions of depositors and the State Bank of India-led shareholders who backed the rescue. It also rounds out a broadly strong earnings season for India's private banks (ICICI, Axis, Kotak all posted gains), a reassuring signal about the health of the financial system that powers the wider economy.
stories.why_now: Indian companies report results every quarter, and July is peak 'earnings season' for banks — Yes Bank's numbers landed alongside those of its larger private-sector peers.
stories.watch_next: Whether Yes Bank can keep its bad-loan ratio low and net interest margin (its lending profitability) rising in coming quarters — and progress on new strategic investors taking bigger stakes.

key_terms.definition: A bank's core earnings: the difference between the interest it collects on loans and the interest it pays out on deposits.
A loan the borrower has stopped repaying; a lower NPA ratio means fewer loans have gone bad.
India's central bank and banking regulator, which sets interest rates and can take over a failing bank.

stories.the_lesson: A bank's health shows up in three numbers together — is it lending more, earning more on those loans, and losing less to defaults? Rising profit alongside a falling bad-loan ratio is the signature of a genuine turnaround.
stories.market_link: Yes Bank shares react to the beat; strong results across ICICI, Axis, Kotak and Yes Bank support the Bank Nifty and overall market sentiment.

stories.title: India moves to add four more Supreme Court judges — a bill to fight the country's mountain of pending cases
key_points: Bill would raise the Supreme Court's judge strength from 33 to 37
Goal: clear a huge backlog of pending cases faster
One of 7 bills the government will table in the monsoon session
Session opens July 20 amid heated opposition protests
stories.background: India's Supreme Court is the country's highest court, and the number of judges it can have is fixed by a law that Parliament can amend. For years the court has struggled with a swelling backlog — tens of thousands of cases wait years for a hearing — because there simply aren't enough judges to keep up with the flood of appeals in a country of 1.4 billion.
stories.what_happened: As Parliament's monsoon session opens on July 20, the government plans to introduce the Supreme Court (Number of Judges) Amendment Bill, 2026, which would raise the sanctioned strength of Supreme Court judges from 33 to 37 (in addition to the Chief Justice of India), with the stated aim of reducing pendency. It is one of seven bills on the agenda, alongside an income-tax overhaul, an MSME (small-business) reform, and others — in a session expected to be stormy over unrelated flashpoints like exam leaks and party defections.
stories.why_it_matters: More judges at the top court means cases — including constitutional questions that affect millions — can potentially be heard sooner, improving access to justice. It also quietly touches a sensitive nerve: how judges are chosen (currently by a judges-led 'collegium'), because more seats means more high-stakes appointments.
stories.ripple_effects: If passed, larger benches and more courtrooms could speed up rulings on everything from citizens' rights to business disputes, which markets and investors watch for legal certainty. It may also revive the long-running tug-of-war between the government and the judiciary over who should have the final say in appointing judges.
stories.why_now: The bill is being tabled at the start of the monsoon session, the government's main window to pass legislation, and reflects mounting pressure over years of case delays that critics say deny 'justice delayed is justice denied' to ordinary litigants.
stories.watch_next: Whether the bill passes cleanly or gets caught in the session's broader opposition standoff — and how the new vacancies would be filled.

key_terms.definition: The pile-up of cases waiting to be heard and decided; a bigger backlog means longer waits for a verdict.
The system where senior Supreme Court judges themselves recommend who becomes a judge, a process the government has long wanted more say in.

stories.the_lesson: When an institution is overwhelmed, the fix can be as basic as raising its capacity — but even a simple 'add more judges' move can reopen deeper fights, here over the age-old question of who gets to choose them.

stories.title: After losing the England series, captain Gill sounds the alarm on India's fitness: 'we're missing a trick'
key_points: India lost the ODI series 2-1 at Lord's
At least five first-choice players missed the decider injured
Star pacer Bumrah played just 2 of 3 ODIs before being ruled out
Gill flags fitness with the 2027 World Cup looming
stories.background: India have just lost a three-match one-day international (ODI) series in England 2-1, with the decider at Lord's. Across the tour the squad was battered by injuries — Hardik Pandya, Nitish Kumar Reddy, Harshit Rana, Washington Sundar and star fast bowler Jasprit Bumrah all missing games. Shubman Gill is India's captain.
stories.what_happened: After the Lord's defeat, Gill publicly questioned the team's fitness, saying a 'minimum five players did not play this match' from the squad originally announced, and that 'if a player is missing after every match, somewhere we are missing a trick.' He said the group — players and the BCCI's medical staff together — must focus on improving fitness, with the 2027 50-over World Cup approaching.
stories.why_it_matters: A captain openly criticising his own squad's fitness is unusual and pointed. Constant injuries force a reshuffled, weakened team, break continuity, and can cost series — exactly what happened in England. It puts workload management and India's medical set-up squarely under the spotlight.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect scrutiny of India's fitness testing and rotation policy, pressure on the BCCI's sports-science team, and renewed debate over players' packed schedules across formats and the IPL. Selection for upcoming series could hinge on fitness as much as form.
stories.why_now: The injury toll peaked in this series — five-plus absentees including Bumrah — and a series loss gave Gill both the reason and the platform to raise it.
stories.watch_next: Whether the BCCI acts on workload management and fitness standards before India's next assignments later in the 2026 season.

key_terms.definition: A one-day international — a limited-overs match of 50 overs per side.
Deliberately resting players, especially fast bowlers, to prevent injury over a long, packed season.

stories.the_lesson: In modern sport, availability is a skill in itself — the most talented squad wins nothing if its best players keep breaking down, so managing bodies is as strategic as picking a team.

stories.title: New Zealand clinch the ODI series in the Caribbean with a nerve-shredding one-wicket win
key_points: Black Caps beat West Indies by 1 wicket in Bridgetown, Barbados
Chasing 188, NZ stumbled to 189/9 — captain Mitchell Santner 34* saw them home
Mark Chapman's 80 held the innings together; Motie took a maiden ODI 5-for (5/47)
The win gives NZ an unbeatable 3-1 lead in the five-match series
stories.background: New Zealand's men's team, the Black Caps, are touring the West Indies for a five-match One Day International (ODI) series — the 50-overs-a-side format. Coming into the fourth match at Kensington Oval in Bridgetown, Barbados, the series was finely poised, and a win would put it beyond West Indies' reach.
stories.what_happened: In a low-scoring thriller on July 19, West Indies made 188, with left-arm spinner Gudakesh Motie's 5 for 47 keeping New Zealand's chase tight. New Zealand wobbled to 189 for 9 but got over the line by one wicket. Mark Chapman's 82-ball 80 was the backbone of the chase, and captain Mitchell Santner finished 34 not out, marshalling the tail — West Indies even dropped a chance with three runs needed before Santner sealed it. That gave New Zealand an unassailable 3-1 lead in the five-match series.
stories.why_it_matters: Winning away in the Caribbean — on a tricky, two-paced pitch where batting was hard — is a real marker of a team's depth and nerve. For West Indies, falling short defending a modest total again points to a fragile batting line-up. Series wins like this feed into ODI rankings and confidence heading toward global tournaments.
stories.ripple_effects: Momentum and ranking points for New Zealand as they build toward future ICC events; more pressure on West Indies' selectors over a batting order that keeps posting under-par totals. Player-of-the-match kind of performances (Chapman, Motie) shape selection debates for both sides.
stories.why_now: The result came in the series decider stage — the fourth of five ODIs — where a single win settled who takes the series, making this the pivotal match of the tour.
stories.watch_next: The dead-rubber fifth ODI — whether West Indies can salvage pride and whether NZ rest key players.

key_terms.definition: A 50-overs-per-side international cricket match completed in a single day.
A series lead so large the trailing team can no longer catch up, even by winning every remaining game.

stories.the_lesson: On a difficult pitch, runs are worth more than they look — a 'small' total can be defendable, and the team that keeps its nerve in a tight chase, not the one with the flashier batting, usually wins.

stories.title: England beat India by 27 runs at Lord's to take the ODI series 2-1 — Duckett's record 141 trumps Rohit's 138
key_points: England posted 387/3 — the highest-ever ODI total at Lord's
Ben Duckett hit a record 141; India fell 27 short despite Rohit's 138
England's first ODI series win over India since 2018
Rohit Sharma brushes off retirement talk: 'Let the noise be there'
stories.background: This was the decider of a three-match one-day international (ODI) series between England and India, level at 1-1: India had won the opener at Edgbaston, England hit back at Cardiff. It was played at Lord's in London — cricket's most famous ground. Extra spotlight fell on India captain Rohit Sharma, 38, whose place in the side and long-term future had been openly questioned going into the match.
stories.what_happened: Batting first, England piled up 387 for 3 — the highest ODI total ever made at Lord's — powered by opener Ben Duckett's record-breaking 141. Chasing 388 to win, India came up 27 runs short, finishing on 360, despite a superb 138 from Rohit Sharma. England's bowlers 'refused to blink' to close out the 27-run win and take the series 2-1 — their first ODI series victory over India since 2018. Afterwards, a defiant Rohit dismissed speculation about retirement: 'Let the noise be there... No noise, no fun.'
stories.why_it_matters: It ends India's recent dominance in this fixture and hands England bragging rights and momentum in the 50-over format. For India, losing a decider they might have won — with the captain scoring a century in a losing cause — sharpens the debate about the team's direction and Rohit's role ahead of major tournaments. Rohit's pointed response signals he intends to keep playing.
stories.ripple_effects: England's batting record (387 at Lord's) and Duckett's form boost their confidence heading into future ODI contests. India's selectors face fresh questions about balance, fitness and succession planning. And the 'will Rohit retire?' storyline — now publicly rejected by the player — will keep running through India's build-up to the next big ICC event.
stories.why_now: The series decider was played on Sunday, July 19; the result and Rohit's post-match comments came through Sunday evening into Monday, which is why it's the top cricket story right now.
stories.watch_next: India's team announcement and captaincy signals for their next assignment — and whether Rohit is retained and in what role.

key_terms.definition: One-Day International — a limited-overs cricket match of 50 overs per side, completed in a single day.
The final, series-deciding match when the teams are level; the winner takes the series.

stories.the_lesson: A standout individual performance doesn't guarantee a team result — Rohit's century and Duckett's record both happened, but the match turned on the 27 runs India's chase fell short by.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported England had piled up a mammoth 387/3 in the Lord's decider, leaving India a huge chase with Rohit Sharma's future in the spotlight.
thread.changed: The chase fell 27 runs short (India 360): England won the match and the series 2-1 — their first over India since 2018 — while Rohit made 138 and afterward brushed off retirement talk.

stories.title: Bangladesh come from behind to clinch the T20 series in Zimbabwe — Tanzid Hasan's unbeaten 66 seals a nervy chase
key_points: Bangladesh won the decider by 4 wickets to take the series 2-1
They fought back after losing the opening match
Tanzid Hasan anchored the chase with an unbeaten 66
A controversial 'dead ball' denied Zimbabwe's Evans a hat-trick
stories.background: Bangladesh and Zimbabwe were playing a three-match Twenty20 International (T20I) series in Bulawayo, Zimbabwe. T20 is cricket's shortest, fastest format — 20 overs a side — and a three-match series is decided by whoever wins two. Bangladesh had lost the first game, meaning the third and final match was a winner-takes-all decider.
stories.what_happened: Chasing Zimbabwe's 143 for 7, Bangladesh reached 144 for 6 with two balls to spare to win by four wickets and take the series 2-1 — their first T20I series win of 2026. Opener Tanzid Hasan held the innings together with an unbeaten 66 off 58 balls, his 13th T20I fifty. The finish was dramatic: an umpire's 'dead ball' call (ruling a delivery void because a fielder was moving illegally) denied Zimbabwe's Brad Evans a hat-trick, and Mahedi Hasan struck the winning boundary.
stories.why_it_matters: For a Bangladesh side that has been inconsistent, bouncing back from 1-0 down to win a series away from home is a confidence boost and a sign of composure under pressure. Series wins abroad are exactly the results teams point to when building toward bigger tournaments.
stories.ripple_effects: Momentum and rankings points from an overseas series win help Bangladesh's standing in the T20I pecking order and give selectors reasons to back their in-form batters. For Zimbabwe, losing a series they led stings and raises questions about closing out tight games.
stories.why_now: The result came Sunday, July 19, in the series-deciding third T20I in Bulawayo.
stories.watch_next: Whether Bangladesh can carry this away-series momentum into their next assignments — and how Zimbabwe respond after letting a lead slip.

key_terms.definition: A short form of cricket where each team faces just 20 overs (120 balls), designed to be fast and high-scoring.
A delivery the umpire rules void — it doesn't count, and no runs or wickets from it stand — often because something illegal or disruptive happened as it was bowled.

stories.the_lesson: Short-format cricket rewards nerve at the death: a single steady innings (Tanzid's unbeaten 66) and one contentious umpiring call can be the difference between winning and losing a whole series.

stories.title: England pile up 387/3 in the Lord's ODI decider — India face a huge chase, and Rohit Sharma's future is on the line
key_points: Series level 1-1; this third ODI at Lord's decides it
England batted first and posted a mammoth 387/3
Ben Duckett hit 141; Joe Root made a record 6th straight ODI fifty
India's chase — and captain Rohit Sharma's place — under the spotlight
stories.background: India and England are playing a three-match One-Day International (ODI) series — the 50-overs-a-side format. India won the first game, England the second, so the sides went to the final match at Lord's in London — cricket's most famous ground — tied 1-1, making it a winner-takes-all decider. Around it swirls a bigger question: the future of India's veteran captain Rohit Sharma, whose place in the one-day side has been debated as selectors look ahead.
stories.what_happened: England won the toss, chose to bat, and piled up a mammoth 387 for 3. Opener Ben Duckett smashed 141, and Joe Root became the first England batter to register six consecutive ODI half-centuries, a record run of form. India's bowlers had a chastening day, and India began their reply needing to chase down a target near 388 — one of the tougher chases in one-day cricket.
stories.why_it_matters: A total near 388 is close to the ceiling of what's achievable in an ODI chase, so England start heavy favourites to take the series. For India, the size of the target — and how the top order, led by Rohit, responds — feeds directly into selection debates about the team's direction.
stories.ripple_effects: If India fall short, expect intensified questions over Rohit Sharma's one-day future and the balance of India's bowling attack. A rare successful chase of this size, by contrast, would be a statement of India's batting depth and take pressure off the captain.
stories.why_now: This is the live, series-deciding match being played today at Lord's — the result settles a tour that has been finely balanced throughout.
stories.watch_next: Whether India's chase of ~388 succeeds or collapses, and the post-match talk on Rohit Sharma's role going forward.

key_terms.definition: One-Day International — a limited-overs cricket match of 50 overs per side, completed in a single day.
The team batting second must score more than the first team's total ('the target') to win; a very high target makes winning much harder.

stories.the_lesson: In a run-chase, the scoreboard sets the psychology: past a certain total the required rate itself becomes the opponent, forcing risks that make failure more likely.

thread.previously: England levelled the series 1-1 in Cardiff (guided by Joe Root's unbeaten 99), setting up a winner-takes-all third ODI at Lord's with Rohit Sharma's future in focus.
thread.changed: The decider is now underway: England batted first and posted a huge 387/3 (Duckett 141, Root a record 6th straight fifty), leaving India a daunting chase to win the series.
